name: Limestone Quarries
short_description: Explore abandoned limestone quarries in the foothills of East Tennessee. Search for calcite crystals, quartz specimens, and feldspar in exposed rock faces and tailings on gently sloped terrain near...
description:
The limestone quarries near Unicoi offer rockhounders access to mineral specimens from Paleozoic limestone formations exposed during decades of commercial quarrying. The site features multiple quarry pits with exposed rock faces and tailings piles at gentle slopes, making it accessible for collectors of varied skill levels. The area sits within the Valley and Ridge province of the Appalachian Mountains, where Ordovician and Silurian age limestone bedrock dominates. Collectors can find calcite crystals in various colors, quartz masses, and feldspar specimens weathering from the host rock. The quarries provide excellent opportunities to observe geological layering and understand the formation of sedimentary minerals in this region.

Best collecting occurs in the exposed pit walls and fresh tailings where weathering has loosened specimens. The gentle slope and accessible terrain make this suitable for families and beginners. Parking is available near quarry entrances, and the site requires no permits for casual collection on accessible areas. Always respect quarry boundaries and any private property markers, as some quarries may still be in use or privately managed.
history: These limestone quarries were developed during the 19th and 20th centuries to extract building stone and aggregate from Ordovician limestone formations that blanket East Tennessee. The mining activity exposed fresh mineral-bearing rock faces, creating excellent collecting opportunities for calcite and other secondary minerals that crystallized within the limestone formations during ancient sea floor deposition over 450 million years ago.
tips: Focus on quarry pit walls where fresh rock faces expose calcite crystals and quartz veins; avoid disturbing loose rock above your head. The pale to white calcite crystals are most visible after rain when the rock faces are wet. Bring a small pry bar or cold chisel to carefully extract specimens from the limestone matrix, and wear safety glasses to protect from rock chips. Check ground-level tailings piles first before climbing higher into pit areas.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1672 feet with gentle slope throughout the quarry complex. Terrain consists of exposed limestone pit walls, loose talus slopes, and old tailings piles. Vegetation is sparse to moderate on slope areas, denser along quarry perimeters. Walking distance from parking to best collecting areas is typically 0.25 to 0.5 miles. Footing is uneven due to loose stone and tailings, requiring sturdy boots.
best_season: April - October. Spring through fall offers the most comfortable collecting conditions with moderate temperatures and reasonable moisture. Summer heat can be intense in exposed quarry pits; fall provides ideal weather for extended field work.
written_directions: From Unicoi, take TN-107 northeast toward the Nolichucky River area for approximately 2.5 miles. Watch for signs or GPS coordinates marking abandoned quarry sites. Access points are typically marked by old pit entrances and overgrown road approaches. Confirm access with locals before entering, as quarry ownership varies.
road_conditions: Approach roads are unpaved and may be rough; high-clearance vehicles recommended for secondary quarry entrances. Primary quarry areas accessible via well-worn paths.
parking_notes: Informal parking available at quarry entrances; no developed lot. Space for 3-5 vehicles near main pit access. Arrive early on weekends.
collection_rules: Casual collection of small hand-specimen sized minerals is generally permitted on exposed quarry surfaces and tailings. Do not remove large blocks or specimens requiring excavation. Respect all private property and quarry boundary markers. Do not enter active mining areas or fenced sections. Pack out all trash. Collection is for personal use only.
rules:
Casual hand-collection only
No commercial harvesting
Respect private property boundaries
Do not excavate or use power tools
Pack out all trash
No collecting in fenced or posted active zones
nearest_services: Unicoi, approximately 2.5 miles south. Gas, food, and water available in town.
accessibility_notes: Gentle slope and relatively flat quarry floor areas make this accessible for most hikers with moderate mobility. Uneven tailings and loose stone require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access to pit areas. Families with young children should supervise closely around pit edges.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at the limestone quarries near Unicoi, Tennessee?
faq.answer: The primary minerals found here are calcite crystals in white, pale yellow, and tan colors, along with quartz masses and feldspar specimens. Calcite is the most abundant; collectors should look for crystal clusters in the exposed limestone pit walls. Small quartz veins occur within the limestone matrix, and feldspar occurs as component minerals in the host rock.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at the limestone quarries in Carter County?
faq.answer: No permit is required for casual hand collection of small specimens from the exposed quarry surfaces and tailings. However, the quarries are private property, so always confirm access is allowed before collecting. Respect any posted signs or boundary markers indicating restricted areas.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect at the Unicoi limestone quarries?
faq.answer: April through October provides the most comfortable collecting conditions. Spring and fall offer moderate temperatures ideal for extended field work. Summer can be hot in exposed pit areas. After rainfall, wet rock faces make calcite crystals easier to spot and extract.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at these limestone quarries beginner-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, this is an excellent beginner site. The gentle slopes, exposed specimens, and easy hand-collection methods make it accessible to families and new rockhounders. Specimens are visible on pit walls and in loose tailings, requiring only basic tools like a cold chisel and pry bar. No special permits or advanced skills needed.

faq.question: What tools and safety equipment should I bring to collect at the limestone quarries?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar or cold chisel, safety glasses, sturdy boots with ankle support, a field bag or backpack, and a magnifying lens. Wear long pants and consider work gloves. Safety glasses are essential because limestone chips easily when extracting crystals. A small hammer is useful but not required.

faq.question: Are dogs allowed at the limestone quarries near Unicoi?
faq.answer: Dogs are generally allowed on these private quarry lands, but keep them under control around pit edges and loose stone. Bring water for your pet, as the terrain offers little shade. Always confirm with landowners or locals before bringing pets, as some quarry areas may have restrictions.

meta_description: Find calcite crystals, quartz, and feldspar at Unicoi limestone quarries. Beginner-friendly private collecting site in Carter County with gentle slopes and exposed specimens.
